title: Core JavaScript language features
summary: The pillars of JavaScript: dynamic typing, first-class functions, prototype-based objects, a single-threaded event-loop concurrency model, and a rich standard library.
content:
## Dynamic typing

Variables have no fixed type; values carry types. Operations may trigger **coercion** (implicit conversion)—know `==` vs `===`.

## Primitives and objects

Primitives: `string`, `number`, `bigint`, `boolean`, `symbol`, `undefined`, `null`. Everything else is an **object** (including arrays and functions). Primitives are immutable; objects are mutable by default.

## First-class functions

Functions are values: assign them, pass them, return them. Enables callbacks, higher-order functions, and closures.

## Prototype-based OOP

Objects delegate missing properties along the **prototype chain** (`Object.getPrototypeOf`). ES2015 `class` is syntactic sugar over prototypes.

## Single thread & concurrency

One **call stack** runs your JS; async work uses queues (callbacks, promises, timers). Long synchronous tasks block UI in browsers.

## Variables and scope

`var` is function-scoped; `let`/`const` are block-scoped and avoid classic loop bugs. **Hoisting** moves declarations; `let`/`const` live in the **temporal dead zone** until initialized.

## Strict mode

`"use strict"` tightens parsing and runtime behavior (e.g. undeclared assignments throw). ES modules are strict by default.

## Errors

`try` / `catch` / `finally`, `throw`. Built-ins include `Error`, `TypeError`, `ReferenceError`, etc.

## Built-in utilities

**JSON**: `JSON.parse` / `JSON.stringify`. **Math**, **Date**, **Intl** for formatting. **RegExp** for pattern matching.

## Closures

Inner functions retain access to outer variables—see the dedicated **Closures** topic.

title: ES6 / ES2015 syntax and values
summary: ECMAScript 2015 introduced block-scoped bindings, arrow functions, template literals, destructuring, spread/rest, default parameters, `for...of`, and enhanced object literals.
content:
## `let` and `const`

**Block-scoped** bindings (nearest `{}`). `const` requires initialization and forbids **rebinding** the identifier (object contents can still mutate unless frozen).

## Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)

From block entry until `let`/`const` execute, accessing the binding throws `ReferenceError`.

## Arrow functions

Concise syntax; **no own `this`**—lexically inherits from enclosing scope. Not suitable for methods that need dynamic `this` unless bound.

## Template literals

Backticks with `${expression}` interpolation and multiline strings. **Tagged templates** pass cooked/raw segments to a function.

## Destructuring

Unpack arrays/objects: `const { a, b } = obj`, `const [x, y] = arr`. Supports defaults and rest patterns.

## Spread and rest

**Spread** expands iterables (`...arr`, object spread). **Rest** collects remaining items (`function f(a, ...rest)`).

## Default parameters

`function greet(name = "Guest")` — defaults apply when argument is `undefined`.

## Enhanced object literals

Shorthand `{ x }` for `{ x: x }`, methods `{ foo() {} }`, computed keys `{ [key]: value }`.

## `for...of` vs `for...in`

`for...of` iterates **values** of iterables. `for...in` enumerates **keys** (including inherited enumerable string keys)—often avoid for arrays.

## Unicode

Better support for astral plane characters in strings and identifiers (`\u{...}`).

## Modules

See **ES6 modules, classes, and built-ins** for `import` / `export`.

code_examples.title: Destructuring and defaults
code_examples.code:
const { name, age = 0 } = user;
const [first, ...rest] = items;

code_examples.title: Arrow functions
code_examples.code:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
const ids = items.map((item) => item.id);

title: ES6 modules, classes, and built-ins
summary: ES2015 added static `import`/`export`, class syntax, Symbol, Map and Set, Promises, iterators, generators, and Proxy/Reflect.
content:
## ES modules

`export` / `import` with static analysis and live bindings. **Default** vs **named** exports. Run in strict mode; bundlers and native `<script type="module">` support.

## Classes

`class`, `extends`, `super`, `constructor`, method shorthand, `static` methods, `get`/`set` accessors. Underlying model remains prototype-based.

## Promises

Standard pattern for async completion (pending → fulfilled/rejected). Chain with `.then` / `.catch`. See also **Promises and async/await**.

## Symbol

Unique opaque values; use for non-colliding property keys (`Symbol.iterator`, etc.).

## Map and Set

**Map** for arbitrary keys with insertion order. **Set** for unique values. WeakMap/WeakSet hold weak object keys for GC-friendly metadata.

## Iterators and generators

**Iterator protocol**: `{ next() → { value, done } }`. **Generators** (`function*`) yield sequences lazily.

## Proxy and Reflect

**Proxy** intercepts object operations (get, set, apply). **Reflect** provides default forwarding helpers—often paired with Proxy.

## Next steps

Modern syntax after ES2015 (optional chaining, `async`/`await`, dynamic `import`) are covered in **Modern ECMAScript (ES2017+)**.

code_examples.title: ES module exports
Class inheritance
Map vs object keys

title: Modern ECMAScript (ES2017+)
summary: Features after ES2015: async/await, object spread, optional chaining, nullish coalescing, dynamic import(), BigInt, and yearly syntax and library improvements.
content:
## ES2017

- **`async`/`await`**: built on Promises; use `try/catch` for errors.
- **Trailing commas** in parameter lists and calls.
- **`Object.entries` / `Object.values`** alongside existing `Object.keys`.

## ES2018

- **Rest/spread for objects** (`{ ...obj }` already common via transpilation).
- **Promise.prototype.finally**.
- **Regex** named groups, dotAll, lookbehind (engine support varies by age).

## ES2019

- **`Array.prototype.flat` / `flatMap`**.
- **`Object.fromEntries`**.
- **`String.prototype.trimStart` / `trimEnd`**.
- **Optional `catch` binding** (`catch { }`).

## ES2020

- **Optional chaining** `?.` for safe property access.
- **Nullish coalescing** `??` (only `null`/`undefined` trigger fallback).
- **`import()`** dynamic module loading (returns Promise).
- **`BigInt`** for arbitrary-precision integers.
- **`globalThis`** uniform global reference.
- **`Promise.allSettled`**.

## Later highlights

- **Logical assignment** (`??=`, `&&=`, `||=`).
- **Class fields** and **private methods** (`#field`) in modern engines.
- **`String.prototype.replaceAll`**, **`Promise.any`**, **`Array.prototype.at`**.
- **Top-level `await`** in modules.

## Using new features

Check **Baseline** / **Can I use** / MDN compatibility tables; transpilers (Babel, SWC) and bundlers target your minimum browsers.

code_examples.title: Optional chaining and nullish coalescing
Dynamic import

title: Closures
summary: A closure gives a function access to variables from its outer (enclosing) lexical scope, even after the outer function returns.
content:
## What is a closure?

A **closure** is created when a nested function captures variables from its parent scope. Those variables persist as long as any reference to the inner function exists.

## Why it matters

Closures power modules before ES modules, memoization, React hooks (conceptually), and event handlers that remember configuration.

## Common pitfalls

- Accidentally sharing loop variables in older `var` loops (use `let` or IIFE).
- Memory: closures keep outer variables alive—detach listeners when done.

code_examples.title: Basic closure
Factory with private state

title: Promises and async/await
summary: Promises model asynchronous completion. async/await is syntactic sugar that makes promise chains read like synchronous code.
content:
## Promises

A Promise is an object representing eventual completion (`resolve`) or failure (`reject`).

## async/await

`async` functions always return a Promise. `await` pauses until the Promise settles *within that async function only*—it does not block the main thread globally.

## Error handling

Use `try/catch` around `await`, or `.catch()` on the promise chain.

code_examples.title: async/await
